title: Fix Footlocker scraper bot detection causing no items on /mens page
description: The Footlocker pagination scraper is being detected by Akamai BMP bot protection, resulting in either zero products scraped or only 1 product saved to jsons/footlocker_mens_products.json. This means the /mens page on the frontend shows no Footlocker listings. The scraper uses undetected-chromedriver but Akamai BMP flags both headless and headed spawned UC sessions. Persistent profile mode (FOOTLOCKER_PROFILE_DIR) did not resolve detection. The FOOTLOCKER_DEBUGGER_ADDRESS attach-to-real-Chrome mode is the most promising fix but needs a reliable workflow (possibly automating the Windows Chrome launch or finding a different approach). The scraper also fails to recover after back() loses the listing page — that secondary failure amplifies the impact.
close_reason: Fixed secondary failure: when back() fails twice, scraper now recovers via direct listing URL nav and switches to force_direct_nav mode for remaining products/pages instead of aborting the entire run

title: Sneaker-name normalization at ingest
description: Each retailer scraper produces a different sneaker name for the same shoe — Salomon=bare 'XT-6 GORE-TEX', Hype DC='Salomon XT-6 Gore-Tex', Platypus='Nike Mens Dunk Low Retro' (gender qualifier prefixed). update_supabase_daily.py upserts sneakers by exact (name, brand_id) match, so retailer-mismatch creates duplicate sneaker rows that the frontend renders as separate cards. Production DB today has 'XT-6' + 'Salomon XT-6', 'XT-6 GORE-TEX' + 'Salomon XT-6 Gore-Tex', 'Nike Air Force 1\'07' + 'Nike Mens Air Force 1 \'07' — all duplicates. Add a normalization pass: strip leading brand prefix, strip 'Mens' qualifier, normalize 'gore-tex'/'Gore-Tex'/'GORE-TEX', casefold for matching. Likely needs a (display_name, lookup_key) split so the rendered string stays nice. Depends on brand canonicalization landing first since the prefix strip uses the canonical brand.

Python: sneaker_lookup_key(scraped_name, canonical_brand) in data_upload/canonicalize.py strips brand prefix, strips leading Mens/Womens, lowercases, strips non-alphanumerics; raises ValueError on empty/brand-only/no-brand input. Backfill ran against 33 production rows, all populated (Updated=33 Skipped=0). update_supabase_daily.py matches by (brand_id, lookup_key) with first-write-wins on display name. 24 tests, all green. Dry-run against the 4 JSON snapshots confirms xt6 / xt6goretex / airforce107 clusters collapse correctly. UNIQUE(brand_id, lookup_key) deferred to xdl. Edge cases (adidas sub-brand words surviving prefix strip, trailing Womens) filed as yv3.

title: Brand-name canonicalization at ingest

The duplicates split the brand filter chips in the frontend and prevent cross-retailer joins on the brand axis. Add a canonicalization step (regex layer or brand_aliases lookup table) between the scraper output and the upsert — must handle case folding, '<brand> brand Logo Large' alt-text bugs, and sub-brand suffixes (Originals/Performance/Sportstyle). Preserve original display strings where useful.

title: check for alignment of names in the supabase db, test different suppliers same shoe actually getting picked up in the db and not displaying differently such as having multiple cards for each show because of some spelling differences, how is this handled in the db, how is the join going to work for the db query write me an explaination and do some testing
design:
## How the join works today (schema-level)

The Supabase schema joins `sneakers ↔ colorways ↔ prices`, with retailer attached on
`prices.retailer_id` and `sneaker_sizes.retailer_id`. There is **no** retailer-scoped
copy of a sneaker — the design intent is that a single `sneakers` row represents one
physical shoe and accumulates retailer-specific data through `prices` / `sneaker_sizes`.
The frontend (`useSneakers` in `aussie-kicks-tracker/src/hooks/useSneakers.tsx`)
renders one card per `sneakers` row and aggregates retailer/price/stock info from
the embedded `colorways → prices` join. Cross-retailer comparison therefore lives
or dies on whether different retailer ingests collapse onto the same `sneakers` row.

## How the ingest decides "same shoe"

`data_upload/update_supabase_daily.py` upserts by **exact** name match:
- `brands`:     `select().eq("name", brand_data["name"])`
- `sneakers`:   `eq("name", sneaker_name).eq("brand_id", brand_id)`
- `colorways`:  `eq("name", colorway_name).eq("sneaker_id", sneaker_id)`

No casefolding, no trimming, no synonym lookup. Two scrapes producing different
strings for the same shoe each create a new row.

## Why retailer ingests do not align

Sampled from `sneaker-scout-backend/jsons/`:
- **Salomon (direct):** bare model, ALL CAPS, no brand prefix — `XT-6 GORE-TEX`,
  colorway `black/black/ftwsilver`.
- **Hype DC:** brand prefixed, Title Case — sneaker `Salomon XT-6`, colorway
  `White / Black / Dusky Orchid`. Brand string is `Salomon brand Logo Large`
  (a scraping bug — alt text of the logo image).
- **Platypus:** brand prefixed, sometimes a `Mens` qualifier — `Nike Mens Dunk
  Low Retro`, colorway `WHITE/BLACK`. Brand is bare (`adidas`, `Vans`).
- **Foot Locker:** brand prefixed, Title Case — `Nike Tuned 1`. Colorway uses
  dashes and trailing nicknames — `Black-Black-Black - BAT`.

The fields collide on three independent axes: brand string, sneaker string, and
colorway string.

## Concrete duplication in production right now

Live DB (2026-05-11) has 33 sneaker rows. A naïve normalization
(lowercase + strip non-alphanumerics + strip leading brand prefix + strip "Mens" +
collapse `gore-tex`/`Gore-Tex`/`GORE-TEX`) collapses to **30 canonical groups**.

The 3 cluster duplicates are:
| canonical | rows in DB |
|---|---|
| `salomon` / `xt 6` | `XT-6` (brand=Salomon) + `Salomon XT-6` (brand=`Salomon brand Logo Large`) |
| `salomon` / `xt 6 goretex` | `XT-6 GORE-TEX` (brand=Salomon) + `Salomon XT-6 Gore-Tex` (brand=`Salomon brand Logo Large`) |
| `nike` / `air force 1 07` | `Nike Air Force 1'07` + `Nike Mens Air Force 1 '07` (both brand=Nike) |

Brands: 13 rows → 9 canonical, with three dup groups:
- `Salomon` + `Salomon brand Logo Large`
- `adidas` + `adidas Originals` + `adidas Performance`
- `ASICS Sportstyle` + `Asics`

User-visible effect: a Salomon XT-6 buyer sees two cards on the listing page (one
backed by salomon.com.au prices, one by Hype DC prices) and the price-comparison
proposition silently breaks. The duplicate adidas brands also split the brand
filter chips.

Also surfaced (out of scope for this issue but worth filing): one Platypus row
is brand-misclassified (`Puma Mostro` filed under `brand=Nike`), and a `Test
Brand` / `Test Sneaker` / `Test Retailer` row pair is polluting prod.

## Recommendation (fixes to file as follow-ups)

1. **Brand canonicalization at ingest** — map raw → canonical via either a regex
   layer or a `brand_aliases` table. Must handle: `<brand> brand Logo Large`,
   `<brand> Originals/Performance/Sportstyle` sub-brand strings, case variants.
2. **Sneaker name normalization at ingest** — strip leading brand prefix, strip
   `Mens` qualifier, casefold for matching but preserve display string somewhere.
   Needs a `display_name` vs lookup-key split, otherwise we lose the nice
   "Nike Air Force 1" rendering.
3. **Colorway normalization** — at minimum casefold and collapse separator
   whitespace before the upsert key check; the rendered string can keep its
   original casing.
4. **Backfill / dedupe migration** — collapse the 3 existing duplicate sneaker
   clusters and 3 brand clusters; reattach orphaned colorways/prices.
5. **Fix Hype DC brand scraping bug** (`Salomon brand Logo Large`) — separate
   issue; the alt text grab is upstream of any canonicalization.

After (1)–(4), `gcg` (cross-retailer search-scraping) and `5qf` (price-drop page)
can rely on cross-retailer rows actually sharing a sneaker_id.
close_reason: Investigation complete — design writeup attached. Filed 5 follow-up issues (1oj brand canon, ery sneaker norm, s8z colorway norm, xdl backfill dedupe, c3o hypedc scraper bug) with dependency edges to 5qf and gcg.

title: Swap hypedc to undetected-chromedriver + manual CAPTCHA fallback
description:
Live diagnostic confirmed HypeDC PDPs are gated by DataDome CAPTCHA (page source returns a 1.5 KB shell with a captcha-delivery.com iframe; title='hypedc.com'). Vanilla selenium + webdriver-manager is exactly the fingerprint DataDome catches.

Plan:
1. Add undetected-chromedriver to requirements.txt.
2. Replace hypedc/pagination_scraper.py setup_driver() with a uc.Chrome() build — UC patches the cdc_* markers, navigator.webdriver, and headless detection automatically, so we drop the manual CDP override and excludeSwitches.
3. Warm up the session once per run by visiting the homepage before any PDP — DataDome scores cookies; arriving on /products/* cold raises suspicion.
4. Detect DataDome blocks in extract_detailed_info() (page_source contains 'captcha-delivery.com' OR title is bare 'hypedc.com' with no h1). On detection in HYPEDC_HEADLESS=false mode, pause via input() and let the user solve the captcha manually — the resulting session cookie carries through the rest of the run.

title: non-atomic reset-then-write for prices.listing_rank uploads
description: Final review of j26 (commit 628dc58 in sneaker-scout-backend) found a real correctness gap in the upload flow. main() in data_upload/update_supabase_daily.py runs _reset_listing_ranks_for_pairs (NULLs the slice) BEFORE insert_or_update_data (writes the new ranks). If insert_or_update_data exceptions mid-loop on item N of M — or per-item try/except blocks swallow errors — the (retailer, gender) slice is left with stale-old ranks gone but only items 1..N-1 of the new ranks written. Until the next successful Hype scrape, the all-view sort is partial. Reset+write should be transactional (Supabase RPC wrapping both) OR adopt staging-column-swap: write new ranks to a staging column first, then atomic-swap with the production column on success. Lower-effort middle ground: defer the reset until AFTER a successful insert_or_update_data, e.g. by tracking 'pairs that ACTUALLY wrote N>0 rows' and only nulling rows whose colorway_id is NOT in the just-written set.
design: Option A: RPC. Wrap _reset_listing_ranks_for_pairs + insert_or_update_data slice in a Postgres function so it's one transaction. Option B: staging swap. Add prices.listing_rank_staging, write new ranks there, then UPDATE prices SET listing_rank = listing_rank_staging where listing_rank_staging IS NOT NULL after the full insert_or_update_data completes successfully. Option C (simplest): after successful upload, NULL only rows whose colorway_id is in the (retailer, gender) slice but NOT in the set of colorway_ids that were written in this run.

title: Mixed-gender listing pages cause PK collision on upload (salomon, possibly others)
description:
When a single retailer listing page contains both mens and womens variants of the same shoe model, the title normalisation strips the 'Men's' / 'Women's' segment so both variants reduce to the same sneaker_lookup_key. The uploader then fails the unique-constraint check on the sneakers table because two distinct gendered rows try to share one PK.

Reproduce: scrape a Salomon listing that exposes both men's and women's variants of the same model (e.g. an outlet/collection page that mixes genders) and run data_upload.run_update against the resulting JSON — the second variant raises a PK conflict.

This likely affects every retailer whose listing page can return both genders simultaneously (the title-normalisation step is shared, not salomon-specific). Reference: salomon/pagination_scraper.py extract_basic_info / combine_product_info, and the upstream name-normalisation helpers in utils/.

Open design questions (defer to plan):
- Should gender be part of the lookup key so the two rows survive as distinct sneakers, OR
- Should the two be folded into one sneakers row with two gendered colorway children (matches the existing colorway.gender column added in sneaker_scout-v7h), OR
- Should we stop stripping gender from the title altogether and let the natural name disambiguate?

title: misalignment of images
Colorway override registry with human-in-the-loop review
description:
The colorway_lookup_key() algorithm strips trailing parens codes, which is correct for most Platypus SKU metadata (e.g. '(108)') but wrong for colorways where the code distinguishes genuinely different products. The NB 9060 has both 'Black' and 'Black (000)' as separate colorways — both collapse to lookup_key='black' and the second one silently merges into the first row.

We need two things:
1. A version-controlled YAML registry (data_upload/colorway_overrides.yaml) where humans document per-sneaker aliases and lookup_key overrides. The ingest pipeline consults this registry first; if a scraped colorway name is in the aliases list, the override lookup_key is used instead of the algorithmic one.
2. A pending-review workflow: when ingest encounters a colorway match where the scraped name is NOT in any known aliases list, it writes the item to jsons/pending_review.jsonl and logs a warning (doesn't block the run). After the run, the human reviews pending_review.jsonl and either adds the name to an existing entry's aliases, or creates a new override entry with a disambiguating lookup_key_override.

## Architecture

### colorway_overrides.yaml schema
```yaml
# Per-sneaker colorway disambiguation, human-reviewed.
# Ingest pipeline (update_supabase_daily.py) checks this registry
# before applying colorway_lookup_key(). If the scraped name matches
# an alias, canonical_name is used as the display name and
# lookup_key_override (if present) replaces the algorithmic key.
sneakers:
  nb9060:   # = sneaker_lookup_key('New Balance 9060', 'New Balance')
    colorways:
      "Black":
        aliases: ["Black", "BLACK", "Black/Black/Black"]
        # no lookup_key_override -> algorithm produces 'black', kept
      "Black (000)":
        aliases: ["Black (000)", "BLACK (000)"]
        lookup_key_override: "black000"  # disambiguate from plain 'black'
```

### Ingest changes (update_supabase_daily.py)
Before computing colorway_lookup = colorway_lookup_key(colorway_name):
1. Look up the current sneaker's lookup_key in colorway_overrides.yaml
2. If found, scan the entries for this sneaker to find which entry contains colorway_name in its aliases list
3. If matched: use that entry's lookup_key_override (or the algorithmic key if none) and canonical_name as the display name
4. If NOT matched but a DB row with the same algorithmic lookup_key already exists: write to pending_review.jsonl and log WARNING
5. If no override exists for this sneaker: proceed with algorithmic lookup_key as today

### pending_review.jsonl format (one JSON object per line)
```json
{"sneaker_key": "nb9060", "sneaker_display": "New Balance 9060", "scraped_colorway": "Black (000)", "computed_lookup_key": "black", "colliding_existing_name": "Black", "retailer": "Platypus", "timestamp": "2026-05-17T10:00:00Z"}
```

### Review tool (data_upload/review_colorways.py)
CLI that reads pending_review.jsonl, shows each item interactively, and updates colorway_overrides.yaml:
- 'same' (s): adds scraped_colorway to the matching entry's aliases list
- 'different' (d): creates a new entry with a disambiguating lookup_key_override (algorithm key + '_' + short suffix)
- 'skip' (x): leaves it in pending for later

## Files
- CREATE: data_upload/colorway_overrides.yaml — initial entries: NB 9060 Black vs Black (000), any other known collisions
- MODIFY: data_upload/update_supabase_daily.py — consult registry before colorway upsert
- CREATE: data_upload/review_colorways.py — interactive review CLI
- MODIFY: tests/test_canonicalize.py — tests for the registry lookup logic
- CREATE: jsons/pending_review.jsonl — generated at runtime, gitignored

## Acceptance criteria
- Re-running hypedc ingest for NB 9060 produces TWO separate colorway rows ('Black' and 'Black (000)') rather than collapsing them
- Any NEW colorway name that would collide algorithmically but isn't in the registry produces a pending_review.jsonl entry and a WARNING log (does not block ingest)
- review_colorways.py lets a human approve/disambiguate pending items and writes the result back to colorway_overrides.yaml
- colorway_overrides.yaml is committed to the repo (the registry IS the human-reviewed dictionary)
close_reason: ColorwayRegistry loads colorway_overrides.yaml; lookup() returns OverrideResult(canonical_name, lookup_key_override) for known aliases. update_supabase_daily.py consults registry before colorway upsert; writes pending_review.jsonl on unrecognised collision. NB 9060 three-black collision (black/black000/black001) resolved in initial YAML. review_colorways.py CLI processes pending items interactively (s=same/d=different/x=skip).

title: Delete stale sneaker_sizes when a size disappears from a retailer PDP
description:
The uploader (both per-row and the new bulk path under 172) only inserts/updates sneaker_sizes rows for sizes present in the current scrape's payload. If a retailer used to stock size 8 of XT-6 black/black but no longer does (the PDP's size button is gone), the existing sneaker_sizes row still says is_available=True. Result: the frontend filter 'show me shoes available in size 8' returns false positives.

Fix: before the sneaker_sizes upsert for a JSON file, DELETE all rows for the (colorway_id, retailer_id) pairs in scope, so the new payload becomes ground truth. One extra round trip per file.

A test for this edge case has been added under 172 marked @pytest.mark.xfail(reason='blocked on this issue') so the regression is documented in CI; the fix here flips the test to passing.

What's needed:
1. Shared helper in utils/ that (a) writes the full exception + traceback + product URL/brand/name/retailer to a per-retailer log file under sneaker-scout-backend/logs/<retailer>_scrape_errors.log, and (b) returns a short classified summary for stdout (e.g. 'renderer timeout after 28.381s', 'element not found: ...', 'parse AttributeError: ...').
2. Wire that helper into every pagination_scraper.py (salomon, hypedc, platypus, footlocker, jdsports) where 'Detail-page error' currently appears.
3. Unit tests for the classifier + that the logger emits a file record.

Acceptance:
- Stdout shows one human-readable line per skipped product (no Chrome stack frames).

- Existing scraper behavior unchanged (skip + continue on detail-page failure).

title: Backfill sizes table after c1p unit-aware ingest
description:
Follow-up to sneaker_scout-c1p. Prior to c1p, the upload script stored every retailer size in uk_size regardless of unit, so existing rows for hypedc/platypus-origin sizes carry a uk_size value that is actually a US value (off by ~1 size) and have eu_size NULL. us_size was also populated as uk_size + 0.5 across the board.

Why this is hard: the sizes table has no source-unit column, so we can't recover the original unit from row content alone. We'd have to re-derive each row's source unit by joining sneaker_sizes -> retailers and looking up the retailer's known default (salomon/footlocker=UK, hypedc/platypus=US), then recompute uk/us/eu and reconcile collisions with rows that are already correct.

Possible approach: a one-shot data_upload/recalc_sizes.py with a --dry-run flag that joins sneaker_sizes to retailers, recomputes canonical uk/us/eu from the retailer's source unit, re-points sneaker_sizes.size_id at the canonical row, and deletes orphans. Alternative: nuke sizes + sneaker_sizes and re-run all retailer uploads from their JSON files.

title: Size unit mismatch: US sizes stored as uk_size in Supabase
description:
parse_uk_size() in data_upload/update_supabase_daily.py strips the numeric value from size strings (e.g. '4 US' -> 4, '8.5 UK' -> 8.5) and stores it in the sizes table's uk_size column regardless of the actual unit. This means a US size 10 gets stored as uk_size=10, but UK 10 and US 10 are different physical sizes (UK 10 ≈ US 11).

Affected scrapers:
- platypus: sizes are 'X US' (US mens)
- hypedc: sizes are 'X US' by default (PDP has US/UK/EU tabs, scraper reads whichever is selected — defaults to US)
- salomon: sizes are 'X UK'

The sizes table has three columns (uk_size, us_size, eu_size) but only uk_size is reliably populated. us_size is set to uk_size + 0.5 as a rough approximation (line 329 of update_supabase_daily.py), which is wrong for both US-sourced and UK-sourced data.

Potential solution:
1. Have each scraper tag sizes with their unit explicitly, e.g. {"value": 10, "unit": "US"} instead of "10 US" string parsing.
2. Add a conversion table or function (US mens to UK is roughly -1, EU to UK is roughly -33.5) to normalize all sizes into uk_size, us_size, and eu_size columns correctly.
3. Update parse_uk_size() to detect the unit suffix and apply the correct conversion before storing. For example: if unit is US, store value in us_size and compute uk_size = value - 1; if unit is UK, store directly; if unit is EU, store in eu_size and compute uk_size = value - 33.5.
4. Backfill existing rows in the sizes table that were inserted with the wrong unit mapping.

Impact: the frontend's size filter and size availability display currently show misleading size labels. A user filtering for UK 10 may see results that are actually US 10 (a full size smaller).
close_reason: Closed

title: HypeDC PDP hydration: extend variant-fieldset wait to 20s + h1 prerequisite
description:
First live run reported 'Variant fieldset didn't render; parsing what's there anyway' for every product, producing JSON with empty sizes/description/colorway. HypeDC's PDP is a Nuxt SSR shell that hydrates the variant grid client-side; the original 8s wait wasn't enough.

Fix: stage the wait (h1 first as a 10s SSR-shell check, then the variant fieldset for up to 20s, with a 0.5s settle for child-label paint). When the fieldset still doesn't appear, log a clearer warning so a future regression is greppable. Also fix is_available in combine_product_info: when hydration timed out and detail dict has no sizes + no price, default to True if the listing surfaced a price (the listing already proved the product exists).

title: Card price = lowest price within active retailer + size filters
description:
Once useSneakers returns a real lowest_price (sneaker_scout-b21), make sure that price respects the active filter set. When the user has filtered to e.g. retailer X + size 9, the displayed price should be min(prices.price) joined to sneaker_sizes where retailer_id IN selectedRetailerIds AND size_id maps to one of selectedSizes — i.e. cheapest among the survivors of the filter, not the global min.

Add a small subtitle on SneakerCardNew like 'from $X · N retailers' so users know the price is filter-scoped, and so a sneaker whose only matching retailer is expensive doesn't look like a great deal at a glance.

Verified via Playwright on /?sizes=4: 3 sneakers shown with prices, each labelled 'across 1 retailer' (Salomon-only DB). XT-6 GORE-TEX correctly drops its 'aspargusgreen' colorway when size 4 isn't available there. Logic also handles multi-retailer case correctly though current Salomon-only data can't visually demonstrate price difference. Unblocks igp.

title: Retailer button on SneakerDetail opens per-product URL (prices.product_url)
description:
At src/pages/SneakerDetail.tsx:422 the external-link button opens retailer.website_url — a generic store homepage. The DB already has prices.product_url (per retailer, per colorway — see src/integrations/supabase/types.ts:125). The button should deep-link to the actual product page so users land on the sneaker they were viewing.

Update fetchRetailersForSneaker in src/lib/sneakerQueries.ts:144 to select product_url alongside price/colorway_id. Pass it through to the visibleRetailers array. Have the click handler open product_url || retailer.website_url (fall back when null, which is fine for retailers we haven't scraped per-product URLs from yet).

Verify by clicking through on a Salomon sneaker — should land on a Salomon product page, not the homepage.
close_reason: fetchRetailersForSneaker now selects prices.product_url and carries it into the retailerMap alongside the chosen price. SneakerDetail external-link button opens product_url || website_url. Verified live: clicking the link on AERO GLIDE 4 detail page captured https://salomon.com.au/collections/mens/products/aero-glide-5 (product page) instead of the homepage https://salomon.com.au — the per-product URL the scraper already populated is now actually used.

title: Replace mock price/stock fields in useSneakers with real DB queries
description:
At src/hooks/useSneakers.tsx:118-120 the hook fabricates lowest_price (Math.random*200+100), price_change (Math.random*40-20), and in_stock (Math.random>0.3). Every consumer of these fields — the price on SneakerCardNew, the In Stock and Price Drops stats on Index.tsx, the on-sale tab — is therefore meaningless.

Replace with real values: lowest_price = min(prices.price) where is_available=true (scoped to active retailer filter when set); in_stock = any sneaker_sizes.is_available=true matching active size+retailer filters; price_change = delta of the two most recent price_history rows per colorway. The reference logic already exists in src/lib/sneakerQueries.ts:fetchAllSneakers but is per-sneaker (N+1 queries). Lift it into useSneakers and batch: one prices query, one sneaker_sizes query, one price_history query for the page's colorway IDs, then reduce in JS.

Supersedes sneaker_scout-2he (which scoped only price_change indicators).
close_reason: Replaced random Math.random() mock for lowest_price/in_stock/price_change in useSneakers with three batched DB queries (prices, sneaker_sizes, price_history) — N+1 collapsed to 3 round trips per page. Verified via Playwright: cards now show real Salomon prices ($259.99-$369.99) instead of random 100-300; size=4 filter narrows to 3 in-stock; size=13 keeps all 7. SneakerCardNew also updated to handle undefined lowest_price gracefully. Unblocks 60q/k6i/4ha.

title: Sidebar size filter is hardcoded [7-12] whole numbers; missing half-sizes and edge sizes
description: FilterSidebar.tsx:15 hardcodes sizeOptions=[7,8,9,10,11,12]. The sizes table actually contains 17 distinct US sizes including halves and edges (4-13). A user wanting to filter by US 9.5 or US 13 has no UI affordance. Fix: add a useSizes hook (parallel to useBrands/useRetailers) returning sorted distinct us_size values, render all of them in the sidebar grid.
close_reason: Added useSizes hook (queries distinct us_size from sizes table). FilterSidebar now renders all 17 DB sizes (US 4 through US 13 incl. halves) instead of hardcoded [7-12]. Validated via Playwright: previously unreachable US 9.5 now filters to 4 sneakers.

title: Make size units consistent: detail page shows UK while sidebar uses US
description: The Available Sizes panel on SneakerDetail renders sizes as '8.5 UK' etc. (reads sizes.uk_size in SneakerDetail.tsx ~201,212), while the listing-page sidebar filter shows 'US 7'-'US 12' and the size filter URL/state is in US. A user who filters by 'US 9' lands on the detail page and sees '8.5 UK' — same physical size, different labels, jarring. Fix: swap detail page to render us_size so the whole UI speaks US consistently.
close_reason: Swapped Available Sizes panel on SneakerDetail to render us_size instead of uk_size, matching the listing sidebar. Validated via Playwright (sizes now display US 8, US 8.5, ..., US 13).

title: Filter prices panel in SneakerDetail by active retailer filter
description: When a retailer filter is active (selectedRetailerIds in FilterContext is non-empty), the SneakerDetail prices panel should only show matching retailers. Read selectedRetailerIds from FilterContext and filter the retailers array before rendering. Add a small inline notice near the panel ('Filtered by N retailer · Clear') so users know filtering is active. Depends on FilterContext existing (sneaker_scout-t2b).
close_reason: SneakerDetail prices panel now filters retailers via selectedRetailerIds from FilterContext. Inline notice shows 'Filtered by N retailer(s) · Clear' (link clears the filter). Cheapest-retailer auto-select constrained to visible retailers. Validated via Playwright (matching id, non-matching id empty state, clear).

title: Add quick supplier filter access from SneakerDetail page
description: Users should be able to adjust retailer filters from within the single shoe page without navigating back. Implement compact inline retailer toggle pills near the prices panel that read from and write to FilterContext. Each pill shows the retailer name and toggles its selection state. Depends on FilterContext existing (sneaker_scout-t2b).
close_reason: Added retailer toggle pills above the Retailer Prices panel on SneakerDetail. Each pill reads/writes selectedRetailerIds via toggleRetailer; aria-pressed reflects state, active pill is filled blue. Validated via Playwright: pill click adds ?retailers=... to URL, second click removes it.

title: useSneakers hardcodes HYPEDC_RETAILER_ID — silent degradation if retailer row recreated
description: aussie-kicks-tracker/src/hooks/useSneakers.tsx:19 hardcodes the Hype DC retailer UUID as a module constant. The backend uploader looks up retailers by name ('Hype DC') so it keeps working through any retailer-row recreation (DB reseed, manual delete+insert). The frontend has no such fallback: if retailers.id changes for the 'Hype DC' row, the per-sneaker Hype rank lookup silently finds zero matches, every sneaker becomes a fallback item, and the all-view sorts entirely by price drop with NO error surfaced. Either fetch the Hype DC retailer ID at query time via a small useRetailers()-style hook cached in React Query (preferred — single source of truth), or add a startup assertion that compares the hardcoded UUID to the live retailers table and logs an error when they diverge. Found in the j26 final cross-cutting review.
design: Preferred: add a tiny useHypeDCRetailerId() React Query hook that selects id from retailers where name='Hype DC', cached indefinitely (retailers don't change). useSneakers consumes it via a hook call at the top, falls back to HYPEDC_RETAILER_ID constant on initial render before the query resolves. Constant becomes a backup, not the source of truth.

title: useSneakers: drop count:'exact', stabilise queryKey, tighten types
description: Three non-blocking observations from reviewing j26's useSneakers rewrite (commit 654675a in aussie-kicks-tracker): (1) the supabase select still passes count:'exact' but the return value uses interleaved.length instead — Postgres runs a wasted COUNT(*) on every fetch; drop the option. (2) React Query queryKey still includes page+limit, so every pagination click triggers a full refetch even though the data is page-independent; move fetch+interleave to a stable queryKey (drop page+limit) and apply slice in React Query's select, mirroring useSaleColorways pattern. (3) Several 'any' casts in the new Hype-rank lookup (data as any[], (c: any), (p: any)) — extract a RawSneakerWithPrices type with { colorways: Array<{ id, gender, prices: Array<{retailer_id, listing_rank}> }> } and drop the casts.
notes: All non-blocking optimisations; defer until catalog grows or hot-reloads become annoying.

title: data_upload uploader cleanup: helpers + log accuracy
description: Four small observations from reviewing j26 uploader commits (ba7fcab, 628dc58): (1) the prices insert and update dicts in insert_or_update_data now share 6 of 7 keys — extract a _price_payload(price_data, price, original_price) helper to prevent future drift; (2) extract the 200 chunk size in _reset_listing_ranks_for_pairs into a module-level _LISTING_RANK_CHUNK_SIZE constant so the URL-cap rationale lives next to the value; (3) the existing 'Inserted price' / 'Updated price' log lines don't mention listing_rank — add it for debuggability; (4) _reset_listing_ranks_for_pairs's docstring says 'logs affected row count' but the code logs len(cw_ids) (colorways considered) — either log the response.data length per chunk or reword the docstring.

title: harden prices.listing_rank: CHECK + index + idempotency
description: Three non-blocking observations from the j26 migration review (commit e40504d in aussie-kicks-tracker): (1) no CHECK constraint enforcing listing_rank > 0 even though the column's contract is 1-based; (2) no supporting index for the planned ORDER BY listing_rank workload — fine at <100 sneakers, file this so we add one when the catalog grows or plans degrade; (3) the migration SQL would error on manual re-run — switching to ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS would be safer for replay scenarios. Address all three when convenient.
design: Suggested follow-up migration: ALTER TABLE prices ADD CONSTRAINT listing_rank_positive CHECK (listing_rank IS NULL OR listing_rank > 0); CREATE INDEX prices_retailer_listing_rank_idx ON prices (retailer_id, listing_rank) WHERE listing_rank IS NOT NULL;

title: set up vitest in aussie-kicks-tracker
description: The frontend has no test runner today (just vite + eslint), so the j26 work shipped src/lib/rankInterleave.ts without unit tests despite being a pure, easily-testable function. Wire up vitest + @testing-library/react, add a CI script, and backfill tests for interleaveStriped (burst/stripe edges, empty queues, mid-stripe exhaustion). Unlocks proper TDD for any future frontend work.
design: Pick vitest (matches Vite ecosystem). Add to package.json devDependencies, add npm script 'test'. First test file should be tests for interleaveStriped — that function was designed in j26 specifically to be unit-testable and is the obvious starter.

title: Sneaker cards misalign vertically when names wrap to 2 lines
description:
On the / listing (and likely /sale, /favorites since they share card components), cards with short 1-line names show the price row higher than neighbour cards with 2-line names. Visible in screenshot — the Vans Classic Slip-On Checkerboard (2-line name) has its $49.99 sitting noticeably lower than the $109.99 / $139.99 / $139.99 in adjacent cards on the same row.

Root cause: SneakerCardNew.tsx:73 uses `line-clamp-2` to cap LONG names at 2 lines, but doesn't reserve the equivalent of 2 lines of height when the name is only 1 line — so the rest of the card content slides up.

Industry-standard fix: give the name container a `min-h` matching exactly two lines of `text-sm` font-size (Tailwind: roughly `min-h-[2.5rem]` for text-sm with default leading), so 1-line names reserve the same vertical real-estate as 2-line ones. Alternative: convert the card body to `flex flex-col` with `mt-auto` on the price row so prices align to the bottom regardless of name height — but min-h on the name is the lower-blast-radius fix.

Apply consistently across SneakerCardNew, ColorwayCard, SneakerCard, SaleColorwayCard, and any per-sneaker card on /favorites. Audit padding/margin to use the same scale tokens (`mb-2`, `mb-3`) across all cards so vertical rhythm stays consistent.
notes: Visual artifact only — no data-correctness issue. Verify by toggling /, /sale, /favorites, and any 4-up grid with a mix of short and long names. Use the Chrome devtools grid inspector to confirm the price row aligns across all cards in a row.

title: CLAUDE.md Docker block references stale single-gender JSON filenames
description: After sneaker_scout-v7h, scrapers emit per-gender JSON (`<retailer>_<gender>_products.json`) and `entrypoint.sh` loops over mens+womens. But the Docker section in `/workspace/CLAUDE.md` still says: (a) 'the loop in entrypoint.sh currently runs ONLY salomon' (line ~63), (b) ad-hoc docker examples upload `backend/jsons/hypedc_products.json` (line ~71 — old singular filename, broken). Update to reflect the new entrypoint loop + per-gender filenames. The 'Run locally (recommended for dev)' block was updated under v7h Task 18 but the Docker block was not — flagged by code reviewer.

title: tests/test_import.py blocks pytest collection
description: `python -m pytest` fails to collect because tests/test_import.py imports `from test import (...)` which resolves to nothing on this layout. Symptom: `ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'test'` followed by 'Interrupted: 1 error during collection'. Workaround used during sneaker_scout-v7h: `pytest --ignore=tests/test_import.py`. The test was discovered during v7h Task 19 gates; full v7h suite (4 new gender tests + existing 132) all pass when this file is excluded. Either repair the imports inside test_import.py to reference the correct module path, or delete the file if it's stale scaffolding.

title: Price-band (min/max) filter on /sale
description: V2 enhancement. FilterSidebar has a Price Range slider that's currently inert; wire it through. Apply post-rollup against SaleColorway.cheapest.price (matches the headline price shown on each card). Deferred from sneaker_scout-5qf.

title: Promote /sale rollup to a Postgres RPC
description: Tracking bead. Today fetchSaleColorways does the rollup client-side; pagination is client-side as a consequence. If query latency exceeds 500ms p95 or the qualifying set grows past a few thousand, define a get_sale_colorways(signal, retailer_ids, sizes, sort, offset, limit) SQL function returning the same SaleColorway shape and swap fetchSaleColorways from .from('colorways')... to .rpc('get_sale_colorways', ...). Hook signature stays unchanged. Deferred from sneaker_scout-5qf.

title: Brand filter on /sale
description: V2 enhancement on /sale. FilterSidebar has a Brands section already; wire the checkboxes through to fetchSaleColorways via an additional .in('sneakers.brand_id', brandIds) clause. Deferred from sneaker_scout-5qf.

title: Add Kids/Junior/Youth qualifier stripping to sneaker_lookup_key
description: The current normalizer (ery) strips leading Mens/Womens/Men's/Women's qualifiers but not Kids/Junior/Youth. 'Nike Kids Dunk Low' and 'Nike Dunk Low' currently produce different lookup_keys ('kidsdunklow' vs 'dunklow'). If JD Sports or Platypus carries kids' shoes, this will create duplicate sneaker rows. Add the qualifier list (Kids/Junior/Youth) to the regex in data_upload/canonicalize.py::sneaker_lookup_key and a covering test. Related to yv3 which captures other ery edge cases.
close_reason: Closed

title: sneaker_lookup_key edge cases: sub-brand words and trailing Mens/Womens
description: ery's sneaker_lookup_key collapses the three known production duplicate clusters but leaves two adjacent edge cases unhandled. (1) Sub-brand words in sneaker names: canonicalize_brand collapses 'adidas Originals' to 'adidas', so the sneaker_lookup_key prefix-strip only removes 'adidas '. The sub-brand word survives into the lookup key: 'adidas Originals BW Army Lux' -> 'originalsbwarmylux'. If another retailer scraped 'BW Army Lux' bare under brand='adidas', the keys would not collapse. Fix: after the brand-prefix strip, also strip a leading sub-brand word (Originals|Performance|Sportstyle). (2) Trailing Mens/Womens qualifier: current regex is leading-only. 'Samba OG Womens' -> 'sambaogwomens'. If another retailer produces 'Samba OG' bare, they would not collapse. Fix: also strip a trailing qualifier. Both gaps are latent, not active today; add covering tests.
close_reason: Closed

title: Combined Hype DC logo-artefact + sub-brand suffix not fully canonicalized
description: canonicalize_brand applies _BRAND_LOGO_SUFFIX and _SUBBRAND_SUFFIX in sequence, both end-anchored. An input like 'adidas brand Logo Large Originals' has the suffix at the end so the logo regex doesn't fire (it's not at $), the subbrand regex strips 'Originals' leaving 'adidas brand Logo Large', which is not in BRAND_ALIASES — so the function falls through with a mangled string instead of canonical 'adidas'. Not currently exploited (Hype DC doesn't emit this combination today as of 2026-05-11) but worth fixing before more retailers land. One-line fix: apply both regexes in a loop until neither matches, or swap order. Add a covering test for the combined input.
close_reason: Closed

title: Add Pydantic models mirroring Supabase canonical schema
description:
The Python backend has dataclasses in utils/models.py that model the scraper's intermediate shape (Product, ProductSize, DetailedProduct), but no canonical type for the Supabase tables the frontend actually reads. The uploader (data_upload/update_supabase_daily.py) currently builds dicts by hand against the table shape, with no validation.

Add a Pydantic module modelling brands, sneakers, colorways, prices, sneaker_sizes, price_history, sizes, retailers, plus a composite SneakerWithPrices type. Mirror the shape from aussie-kicks-tracker/src/integrations/supabase/types.ts so Python and TS agree. Add pydantic to requirements.txt. Leave the existing dataclasses alone — they model scrape-time scratch space and have three importers.
close_reason: Closed

title: Auth-gate /admin (follow-up to admin page)
description:
Once /admin exists (sneaker_scout-jwl) and is shipped public, lock it down. Simplest path: use the existing useAuth hook in src/pages/Admin.tsx — when no user, redirect to /auth?next=/admin and return after login. No role check yet — any signed-in user can see admin stats.

If a stricter role check is wanted later, that's a separate decision (would need a profiles.is_admin column or similar). File this as a low-priority follow-up so /admin can ship and be validated first.

Depends on sneaker_scout-jwl (the page must exist before it can be gated).
close_reason: Closed

title: Add 'split colorways' toggle on listing grid (Grouped default, Split opt-in)
description:
Different colorways of the same sneaker can have different lowest prices and different stock states across retailers. Today the listing groups colorways under one card showing only the primary colorway's image and a single price — a colorway with a steeper discount or unique availability gets hidden.

Add a toggle in the listing header (next to grid/list view buttons) that switches between:
- **Grouped** (default — current behaviour): one card per sneaker, primary colorway image, swatch row showing siblings.
- **Split**: one card per colorway. Each card carries that colorway's own image and its own filter-scoped lowest_price (depends on sneaker_scout-60q). Click navigates to /sneaker/:id?colorway=<colorwayId>; SneakerDetail reads the query param and calls setSelectedColorway to preselect.

Persist the toggle in URL (?view=split) so it survives reload and is shareable. Default stays Grouped — least disruptive to existing users.

Depends on sneaker_scout-60q (need colorway-level lowest_price for split cards to be meaningful).
close_reason: Closed

title: Move stats + Price Trends to /admin; show Total Sneakers + Total Colorways on Index
description:
The In Stock and Price Drops tiles plus the Price Trends panel on Index.tsx are admin-y noise for the public listing — the user wants them moved to a dedicated /admin page once they show real data (sneaker_scout-k6i, sneaker_scout-4ha).

Steps:
1. Create src/pages/Admin.tsx and add <Route path='/admin' element={<Admin />} /> in App.tsx.
2. Move the In Stock / Price Drops cards from Index.tsx:71-93 to Admin.
3. Move the Price Trends Card block at Index.tsx:104-131 to Admin, and replace the three hardcoded mockPriceData PriceCharts (Nike Dunk / Air Jordan / Yeezy — not even brands we have!) with real data: top three sneakers ranked by recent price drop magnitude (or by price_history activity), each rendered as a PriceChart fed from price_history rows.
4. On Index, replace the 3-tile row with a 2-tile row: Total Sneakers (existing count) and a new Total Colorways tile. Add a useColorwaysCount hook beside useSneakers that counts colorways whose sneaker_id survives the active filter.

/admin ships PUBLIC for now — auth gating is filed separately as a follow-up.

Depends on sneaker_scout-k6i and sneaker_scout-4ha (no point moving fake stats).
close_reason: Closed

title: Real In Stock stat (count over full filtered set)
description:
Index.tsx:35 does inStockSneakers = sneakers.filter(s => s.in_stock).length. Same two problems as Price Drops: pre-b21 in_stock is random, and post-b21 the count would still only cover the current page.

After sneaker_scout-b21, replace with an aggregate query: count distinct sneakers that have at least one sneaker_sizes row with is_available=true matching the active retailer+size filter. Surface as the 'In Stock' tile (which after sneaker_scout-NEW5 lives on /admin).

Depends on sneaker_scout-b21.
close_reason: Added useInStockCount and usePriceDropsCount hooks issuing aggregate queries over the full filter-scoped set. useInStockCount uses count:exact head:true on sneakers with inner-join through colorways→sneaker_sizes (is_available=true, plus retailer/size when filtered). usePriceDropsCount fetches matching sneaker+colorway IDs, then their price_history (desc), then computes per-sneaker latest-vs-prior delta in JS and counts negatives. Index.tsx tile values now come from these hooks instead of page-level filter; the 'On Sale' tab keeps a page-scoped count (priceDropsOnPage) since its contents are also page-scoped. Verified live: unfiltered shows 7/7/0; ?sizes=4 narrows to 3/3/0; direct DB call confirms count=7. Both unblock jwl.

title: Real Price Drops stat (count over full filtered set)
description:
Index.tsx:36 currently does priceDrops = sneakers.filter(s => (s.price_change || 0) < 0).length, which (a) is random mock pre-b21, and (b) only counts the current page even after b21 lands.

After sneaker_scout-b21 wires real price_change, replace this with an aggregate count over the *full filtered set* (not just the current page of 20). Issue a separate count query that joins price_history twice (most-recent and second-most-recent per colorway/retailer) and counts sneakers where the delta < 0 across all retailers/sizes the active filter allows. Surface as the 'Price Drops' tile (which after sneaker_scout-NEW5 lives on /admin).

Depends on sneaker_scout-b21.
close_reason: Added useInStockCount and usePriceDropsCount hooks issuing aggregate queries over the full filter-scoped set. useInStockCount uses count:exact head:true on sneakers with inner-join through colorways→sneaker_sizes (is_available=true, plus retailer/size when filtered). usePriceDropsCount fetches matching sneaker+colorway IDs, then their price_history (desc), then computes per-sneaker latest-vs-prior delta in JS and counts negatives. Index.tsx tile values now come from these hooks instead of page-level filter; the 'On Sale' tab keeps a page-scoped count (priceDropsOnPage) since its contents are also page-scoped. Verified live: unfiltered shows 7/7/0; ?sizes=4 narrows to 3/3/0; direct DB call confirms count=7. Both unblock jwl.

title: Implement real price change indicators on listing cards
description: The price_change value in useSneakers hook is a random mock - the TrendingUp/TrendingDown arrows on listing cards are meaningless. Replace with real calculation: query price_history for the two most recent entries per colorway per retailer, compute the delta, and surface as a real signed price_change value. Also fix the Price Drops stat in Index.tsx to count real changes. Additionally, guard the detail page price history arrow to only render when real priceHistory rows exist. This is deprioritised - do after higher-priority issues.

title: generalise rankInterleave beyond Hype-specific naming
description: Observation from reviewing j26's interleaveStriped (commit 47dd0f5 in aussie-kicks-tracker): RankedSneaker.hypeRank hardcodes Hype DC semantics in the type. If we ever want a Platypus-mirror or Foot-Locker-mirror variant, the field will need renaming to primaryRank (or the function needs to take a key selector). Not needed now — j26 IS the Hype mirror — but worth a note so future work doesn't fork the function. Also: replace the '(a.hypeRank as number)' cast in the sort with a typed predicate ('const hasRank = (r): r is RankedSneaker & { hypeRank: number } => r.hypeRank !== null') to eliminate the cast.
notes: Defer until a second mirror variant is requested.

title: hypedc scraper test polish: monkeypatch helper + edge coverage
description: Two minor observations from reviewing j26 scraper tests (commits 8ed2b8a, 8340ff6, 8642132): (1) test_scrape_via_static_fallback_assigns_running_ranks uses the cryptic 'lambda: (_ for _ in ()).throw(...)' idiom to stub setup_driver — cleaner with 'def _no_chrome(): raise RuntimeError(...)' then monkeypatch.setattr; (2) the live scrape_all_pages per-product loop with its two 'continue' branches (missing URL, detail-page error) isn't directly tested — add a stubbed test that injects a URL-less entry and asserts the skip-doesn't-consume-rank invariant. (3) test_update_supabase_rank_pairs lacks coverage for retailer_name='' or gender='' — one extra assertion would lock that branch.

title: Refuse to upload if any sneakers.lookup_key is empty (deployment guard)
description: ery's invariant is that every sneakers row has a non-empty lookup_key — this is required for the upsert SELECT to behave correctly. The invariant is established by running the backfill (data_upload/backfill_lookup_keys.py) AFTER the schema migration but BEFORE the wiring change ships. There is no automated guard preventing someone from skipping the backfill on a fresh DB or a forked environment. Add a check at the start of data_upload/run_update.py (or update_supabase_daily.py main()) that queries 'SELECT count(*) FROM sneakers WHERE lookup_key = empty-string' and refuses to proceed if the count is non-zero. Pointer to data_upload/backfill_lookup_keys.py in the error message.

title: canonicalize_brand: unicode/typography normalization
description: canonicalize_brand uses s.lower() for BRAND_ALIASES lookup but does no unicode normalization (NFC/NFKC), so scrapers that produce non-ASCII lookalikes — typographic apostrophes, non-breaking spaces, full-width letters — would fall through as unknowns. Not currently observed in any scraper output but worth handling if a future retailer pulls from a structured data feed. Add unicodedata.normalize('NFKC', s).strip() before the lookup, plus a test covering at least one non-ASCII case.
